main_summary.summary: Steven McNichols, interviewed in March 2013 in Berkeley, California by David Cline for the Civil Rights History Project, recounts his trajectory from a New York childhood split between a 133rd Street tenement and a Dalton School scholarship to a UCLA-based civil rights career. McNichols describes participating in the September 1961 Houston Freedom Ride organized by Houston attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K., bankrolled in part by Theodore Hugo Brooks and supported by the Progressive Youth Association. He recounts the group of eighteen demonstrators arrested for unlawful assembly at the Union Station coffee shop and the overnight beating administered by white prisoners in the Harris County misdemeanor tank under jail trustees Garland and Omer Street. After conviction was reversed by the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals, McNichols became Civil Rights Director of the National Student Association, organizing the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom that financed expansion of the Department of Agriculture surplus food program across every Mississippi county. He describes lobbying for the 1964 Civil Rights Act and Voting Rights Act through the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights, work on the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee with Rita Schwerner, a McComb, Mississippi visit at the height of the 1964 bombings, and his retrospective view that NSA's international apparatus was a CIA front directed by Bob Kiley.
key_themes: National Student Association civil rights work
main_summary.historical_significance: McNichols offers detailed first-person testimony on the Los Angeles CORE chapter of the 1961 Freedom Rides, a less-documented West Coast extension of the campaign organized by Arnold Walters's LA CORE and Houston attorneys George Washington Jr. and Hayma K. His account of the overnight beating in the Harris County misdemeanor tank, the role of trustees Garland and Omer Street, the L.A. Times article that triggered Garland's near-retaliation, and George Washington Jr.'s Polaroid documentation of injuries fills a gap in the Houston Freedom Ride record. His subsequent NSA work documents how the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom funded the Department of Agriculture surplus food rollout to every county in Mississippi, a poverty-relief mechanism not centered in most Civil Rights Movement narratives. The interview also preserves contemporaneous observations on the NSA-CIA pipeline (Bob Kiley to Richard Helms) and on AFL-CIO anticommunist resistance to SNCC within the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights.

chapters.part: A Family From Tel Aviv to Harlem
chapters.title: Founders of Tel Aviv
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Berlin, California, in March, Steve McNichols opens on his origins, maternal grandparents who emigrated from Eastern Europe to Palestine in 1890 under a Rothschild-sponsored movement and were among the families who founded Tel Aviv, where his mother was born before the family moved to Geneva and she became a classically trained violinist who toured with Joseph Szigeti.

chapters.part: A Family From Tel Aviv to Harlem
chapters.title: The Only White Kid on 133rd Street
chapters.topic: A Father and a Tough Neighborhood
chapters.summary: Born in New York in 1939, McNichols describes his father, raised in a tough St. Louis neighborhood by a mother murdered when the father was three, a smart, self-educated man, and his own childhood as the only white kid on 133rd Street in a poor Puerto Rican neighborhood while attending the Dalton School on full scholarship alongside children of the wealthiest families in the country.

chapters.part: A Family From Tel Aviv to Harlem
chapters.title: Get the Hell Out
chapters.summary: He recalls being conscious of race and class while shuttling between a private school and the ghetto, his mother driving him to succeed so he would escape her kind of life, and his father, who worked an animal-lab job then taught himself electronics to become a top trainee repairman for an X-ray corporation despite peers holding engineering degrees.

chapters.part: A Family From Tel Aviv to Harlem
chapters.title: A Breakdown in the Time-Life Lobby
chapters.topic: His Mother's Illness and His Own
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts his parents separating, his mother prospering by teaching French to Time-Life employees before developing psychiatric symptoms and a breakdown in the Time-Life lobby that left her institutionalized, his own depression and suicidal ideation from age fifteen, and the bipolar condition undiagnosed until age sixty-five when lithium finally lifted it.

chapters.title: Play Tennis, Go to UCLA
chapters.summary: He describes the years after his mother's hospitalization, drifting apart from his father, and, asked at eighteen what he would do after Stuyvesant, declaring he would go to Los Angeles to play tennis and attend UCLA, then driving to Denver and on to LA where he took a room at the YMCA and put himself through UCLA.

chapters.title: They Blew the Operation
chapters.summary: McNichols tells how, years later, his mother's untreated brain tumor left her unable to walk, and how surgeons removed it but failed to replace it so the walls of the brain collapsed and she died half an hour after the operation, the end of a cultured, educated woman who he says never had a chance.

chapters.title: A CIA Front for Students
chapters.summary: He recounts entering UCLA in 1958, feeling alien among middle-class students, plunging into campus politics with figures like Howard Berman and Henry Waxman, and rising in the National Student Association, only to find its international Cold War work against a communist student union was a CIA-funded network whose head Bob Kiley had been an NSA president.

chapters.title: I Wanted to Be a Freedom Rider
chapters.summary: McNichols describes being electrified by news of the sit-ins, which reached Los Angeles through an East-to-West pipeline before the LA Times covered the movement, his disbelief that Black people could be refused service at lunch counters, and his and fellow UCLA students' move into demonstrations outside Kresge's stores.

chapters.title: Two Black Attorneys in Houston
chapters.summary: He recalls struggling to get apathetic UCLA students involved, then explains how the Houston Freedom Ride was arranged by two driving Black civil rights attorneys in Houston, one named George Washington Jr., who challenged the local Black establishment and formed their own club, joined by a third figure he describes as a businessman and fence who put up the cash.

chapters.title: Send Us Some Freedom Riders
chapters.summary: McNichols tells how the Houston organizers called James Farmer, head of CORE and an original Freedom Rider, offering to cover costs and provide counsel if riders were sent, how Los Angeles CORE took up the call, and how he, a UCLA student, learned of it and volunteered for what became the Houston Freedom Ride.

chapters.title: They Never Showed Up
chapters.summary: He describes the LA CORE director, challenged by ambitious rivals, two of whom loudly vowed to join the Houston ride but vanished from the platform, and the two days of training in someone's home that focused entirely on being attacked by a white mob at the station and offered almost no help for what to do after arrest.

chapters.title: The Wrong Railroad
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts how a CORE member bought tickets on Southern Pacific without knowing which line served Houston, that the destination station there had replaced its lunch counter with vending machines, and how a group of eleven riders, including a Berkeley man elected to lead them, set out for Houston as part of the West Coast student movement.

chapters.title: An FBI Agent Joins the Car
chapters.summary: He describes the train trip as uneventful except for a large, well-dressed FBI agent who boarded at El Paso, whom the Black porters had warned them about, and who, claiming to work for the railroad, sat near the riders for the rest of the journey to Houston.

chapters.title: Houston Was the Target
chapters.summary: McNichols tells of arriving in Houston to find no lunch counter, being picked up by the attorneys and the local progressive student association, and clarifies that, contrary to those who think they were always bound for Jackson, Houston itself was the target where they aimed to challenge Texas segregation statutes and be arrested.

chapters.title: Soul Food and a Held Door
chapters.topic: The Local Allies and First Sit-In
chapters.summary: He recalls his first soul-food meal at a Black restaurant after being picked up, joining forces with the progressive student association, and the union-station lunch counter where the racist owner held the door shut against the demonstrators until he stepped away so he could have them arrested.

chapters.title: The Waitresses Were All for Us
chapters.summary: McNichols describes the eighteen well-dressed demonstrators in coats and ties forming a line, walking in to sit at the counter and a booth when the owner stepped aside, and the white waitresses who whispered support but could not say so openly for fear of losing their jobs as a crowd gathered and police were called.

chapters.title: I Wasn't Nonviolent
chapters.summary: He recalls a hostile crowd and threats outside, then reflects that while CORE was genuinely committed to nonviolence, he himself, raised in tough New York, was not, adhering to nonviolent standards out of devotion to CORE and the Freedom Ride rather than conviction.

chapters.title: Singing in the Bar Car
chapters.topic: The Ride's Spirit and the Arrest
chapters.summary: McNichols tells how, warned to stay out of the bar car and not sing freedom songs, the riders were singing there within fifteen minutes, his growing fear before the action, a local student calming him, and the moment of arrest when one rider's defiant announcement set even the threatening white onlookers laughing before they were driven carefully to jail.

chapters.title: Sorted Into the Tanks
chapters.summary: He describes the booking at the county jail, where prisoners were pulled from the holding cells and placed in segregated tanks, white men, white women, and Black prisoners each in their own, the riders booked around two in the morning, and a fellow rider named Steve talking back to the processing officer in a way McNichols feared would invite a beating.

chapters.title: One of Those Nigger Lovers
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts being put in the white male misdemeanor tank and ordered by a deputy to tell the prisoners what they were arrested for, his naive announcement of unlawful assembly, a prisoner's slur, and being quickly surrounded by tough men and walked to the back of a tank of a hundred and seven prisoners and told to lie down and take off their shoes.

chapters.title: Lie Still and Take It
chapters.summary: He describes the beating once they lay down, a sadistic prisoner pounding Steve's ribs and others kicking him, a man landing on McNichols and beating him until he lay still as his ghetto youth had taught him, while the youngest rider, Joe Stevenson, flipped onto his stomach and was overlooked, the prisoners then circling and threatening through the night.

chapters.title: Dawn in the Tank
chapters.summary: McNichols catalogs the injuries, his own hurt knee, a two-inch wound behind one rider's ear, another kicked in the eye, and explains the guards had told the prisoners to beat them, then describes the slow coming of dawn, coffee and breakfast arriving, and the riders being gradually allowed to stand among the hundred and seven prisoners.

chapters.title: A Prisoner Offered His Fork
chapters.summary: He recalls being treated decently once breakfast came, prisoners approaching individually and some claiming to support them though they would not when it counted, and one inmate who offered McNichols a carved fork as a gesture of sympathy that he hesitated to accept, a sign that some were watching what the riders were doing.

chapters.title: The Commander Called Garland
chapters.summary: McNichols explains CORE's plan was to bail out rather than serve time, which they did after two days as their lives were threatened, and describes the misdemeanor tank ruled by a clique under a head convict called the commander, Garland, in cell number one, and his lieutenant Omer Street who roamed the overcrowded tank as the strong arm of the guards.

chapters.title: The Most Dangerous Man in the Jail
chapters.summary: He recounts the prisoners insisting the riders were lucky to be alive, and the most dangerous inmate, a man arrested for murder who had cut three attackers in a felony tank, kept in solitary six days a week and brought to the misdemeanor tank once a week so he would not go crazy, deferring even to Garland after a brief challenge before being returned to isolation.

chapters.title: Shoes for Protection
chapters.summary: McNichols describes other criminals in the tank, a violent pimp and professional thieves, the tense second night when the sadist again wanted to beat them, and how Omer Street, who had asked McNichols for shoes, recognized his fear and struck a quid pro quo, announcing he wanted nothing to happen to the riders in exchange for the shoes.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: He Took the Article Personally
chapters.topic: An LA Times Story Reaches the Jail
chapters.summary: He recounts Garland confronting him for being called a serenade, and a copy of an LA Times article about the arrest reaching the tank that Garland took personally, fearing it identified him, until the attorney George Washington Jr., visiting the white realms last, asked one beaten rider what had happened and was shown the wound behind his ear.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: I Thought We Were Dead
chapters.summary: McNichols describes Garland, already blamed for the beating, going berserk and running screaming around the tank threatening them again, the attorney having a beaten rider pulled out to a consultation cell, and how the youngest rider sat down and calmly studied Garland while McNichols was sure they would be killed, until guards and the supervisor talked Garland down over twenty minutes.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: Bonded Out at Midnight
chapters.summary: He tells of being moved to an isolation cell and finally bonded out after the situation calmed, an argument with the youngest rider who wanted to stay rather than worry about being beaten, retrieving their clothes and money, and walking out into the parking lot while the other three riders remained in their separate tanks unaware of what had happened.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: Driving Around for Bail Money
chapters.topic: Charges and the Wait for Trial
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts being taken to the businessman's home near midnight to recount the experience over a drink, being charged with unlawful assembly, all the riders released within about a week, and spending three weeks driving around making club speeches to raise funds while limited local media let the sheriff's denial of the beating outweigh their account.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: Union Center or Union Coffee Shop
chapters.summary: He describes the first trial ending in a mistrial after the district attorney dropped Union Center to avoid an interstate-commerce question, the attorney George Washington's thorough motion that the charge naming the wrong coffee shop created a fatal conflict, and a newly appointed conservative judge who, realizing the attorney was right, phoned for guidance and dismissed the case.

chapters.part: George Washington Jr. and the Trial
chapters.title: The Difference Between Texas and Mississippi
chapters.summary: McNichols contrasts Texas with Mississippi, noting the Houston attorneys and judges accommodated the riders by setting a quick trial date so they could return to register for school, that the lawyers feared offending judges they would work with again, and how at the second trial a hostile jury convicted them as they kept a poker face, leaving them free on appeal.

chapters.part: Home, the Oral History, and the Appeal
chapters.title: A Bitter Train North
chapters.summary: He recounts leaving on Union Pacific as bitter men after the conviction, two riders having pressured Black women travelers in Houston for sex which upset the women, and how, having been beaten and convicted without justification, the group fought with the porters on the way home instead of working with them as they had going down, with no freedom songs this time.

chapters.part: Home, the Oral History, and the Appeal
chapters.title: Steve, Stop Talking
chapters.summary: McNichols describes the Freedom Ride ending back at Union Station, a professor arranging for him to record an oral history at the UCLA project, his account so detailed it filled four hundred pages and a typist worked for weeks, and his later return to Houston at his own expense to learn more about the businessman's bail bond and the progressive youth association organizers.

chapters.part: Home, the Oral History, and the Appeal
chapters.title: A Hernia, a Disk, and Mr. Freedom Rider
chapters.summary: He tells of digging into the Houston organizers' backgrounds and their ties to a Senate candidate, failing to get the group's leader to sue the sheriff because no law yet supported it, and returning to UCLA where, nursing a painful ruptured disk, he leveraged his standing as a beaten Freedom Rider, the only one on the West Coast, into a leadership role.

chapters.part: Home, the Oral History, and the Appeal
chapters.title: The Only One on the West Coast
chapters.summary: McNichols reflects on how being known as a Freedom Rider beaten in jail reinforced his standing so that anyone passing through Los Angeles checked in with him, his loose ongoing contact with CORE and James Farmer, and the appeal reaching the Texas Court of Criminal Appeals, which reversed the convictions on a Texas statute barring race discrimination in transportation that a well-educated Black rider had found.

chapters.title: Civil Rights Director in Philadelphia
chapters.summary: He describes balancing the National Student Association with the movement over several years, a bad Congress where he failed to win national office, and instead becoming the NSA civil rights director and moving to Philadelphia, his first event a conference in Atlanta and a drive to McComb, Mississippi, where there had been eleven bombings in twenty-two days after the murders of the summer.

chapters.title: The Closer to Jackson, the More Scared
chapters.summary: McNichols recounts flying into Meridian and growing more afraid the closer he got to Jackson, his year of nightly dreams reliving the beating, checking in with SNCC and driving to McComb for a rally, and being escorted out at four in the morning by a police officer who put his hands on his head and told him not to come back before he flew home.

chapters.title: The Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom
chapters.summary: He describes raising thirty-eight thousand dollars through the 1964 Thanksgiving Fast for Freedom to buy canned goods for Mississippi, then, feeling he had exploited the movement and wanting to do right, conceiving a scheme to petition the Department of Agriculture and partnering with the National Council of Churches Commission on Religion and Race, Robert Spike, and the Delta Ministry's Art Thomas.

chapters.title: Knocking Off Counties
chapters.summary: McNichols explains the gambit by which a surplus-food official, threatening to route distribution through civil rights groups no county wanted, pressured county after county to enroll in the federal surplus food program, so that by spring every county in Mississippi had joined, raising rural participation from a low fraction to a hundred percent and getting food to Black people who could not get it before.

chapters.part: The Leadership Conference and the MFDP
chapters.title: Books and the White House Pen
chapters.topic: Civil Rights and Voting Rights Acts
chapters.summary: He recounts a second program, Books for Equal Opportunity, sending donated books to Mississippi, the NSA's deep involvement in the Leadership Conference on Civil Rights and passage of the 1964 Civil Rights Act and the Voting Rights Act, his invitation to a White House signing ceremony where he received one of the pens, and the resentment he stirred by always pushing the conference to do more.

chapters.part: The Leadership Conference and the MFDP
chapters.title: Rita Schwerner, a Conscience
chapters.topic: The MFDP and a Widow's Resolve
chapters.summary: McNichols describes spending time in New York on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party effort to organize Congress members to seat the MFDP and unseat the regular Mississippi delegation, and meeting Rita Schwerner, widow of the murdered Mickey Schwerner, who became close to him and a militant conscience, stronger and more committed than he was.

chapters.part: The Leadership Conference and the MFDP
chapters.title: On the Strategy Committee
chapters.summary: He recalls becoming a member of the MFDP Congressional Strategy Committee, going to Washington weekly for urgent strategy meetings, arranging through the National Council of Churches to spend two weeks investigating the challenge, and establishing that the White House had worked behind the scenes to oppose it, a fact then unknown.

chapters.part: The Leadership Conference and the MFDP
chapters.title: They Were Convinced They Were Communists
chapters.summary: McNichols closes on his lament that the AFL-CIO and NAACP leadership in the Leadership Conference, populated by veterans of 1930s labor organizing who had fought the communists, adamantly opposed the SNCC and movement people as communists, many of whose parents had been, a divide he found shameful but true.

loc_video.caption: Steven McNichols oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Burlingame, California, 2013 March 01
